expert: Kellie Rados — UX designer specializing in animal-welfare organizations (Best Friends Animal Society redesign)
integration_assessment: The integration is real but one-way and lossy — a funnel that destroys its most valuable cargo. What works: the app manages the website's animal listings (AnimalDetail's WebsiteListingTab via the functions/api/site proxy), auction items flow from AuctionItemsPage to fundraiser.html, and website contact submissions surface in the app's Inbox (stores/contacts.js). What fails: (1) The website's rich multi-step adoption/foster applications are flattened into a 4-column "contacts" row at the API boundary — _worker.js's POST /contact stores only name/email/subject/message, silently discarding phone, address, experience, living setup, and every other field the applicant carefully filled in. (2) The Inbox "Send reply" button only writes a `reply` column to Neon (contactsService.js PATCH) — no email is ever sent, so staff believe they've responded and applicants never hear back. (3) The app's ApplicationsPage/AdoptionsPage read a separate Firestore `applications` collection populated by seed scripts — real website applicants never reach the "Applications" workflow, so the approve/deny pipeline operates on phantom data. (4) Donations (Zeffy links) and newsletter signups bypass the app's donors store entirely — the donor CRM has no live inflow. The front door exists; the hallway behind it is disconnected from the rooms.

top_findings.title: Adoption/foster application data is silently discarded at the API — staff cannot honor the '48-hour call' promise
top_findings.evidence: adopt-apply.html lines 464-509 collects 11 required fields (phone, address, experience, setup, other-animals, care, goals...) and promises 'Within 48 hours: We'll call'. It submits via SF.sendContact (app.js line 37) to POST /api/contact, but _worker.js (~line 14655) inserts only name, email, subject, message into contactsTable. foster-apply.html has the same pattern.
top_findings.detail: The applicant's phone number — required in the form, essential to the promised follow-up call — never reaches storage. Same for their address, animal-care experience, and living situation. Staff reviewing the Inbox see only the free-text 'Anything else we should know?' answer. Every adoption application since launch has arrived amputated, and the emotional contract set by the beautifully written confirmation message ('We'll call to discuss the animal you're interested in') is structurally impossible to keep.

top_findings.title: Inbox 'Send reply' never contacts the applicant — a false affordance that strands adopters
top_findings.evidence: SB- v2/src/services/contactsService.js replyToContact() PATCHes {reply: text} to the site API; SB- v2/src/components/features/Inbox.vue shows 'Send reply' / '✓ Replied' pills. No email-sending code exists anywhere in _worker.js (no MailChannels/Resend/SMTP).
top_findings.detail: A staffer taps Reply, writes a warm response, sees 'Sending…' then '✓ Replied', and moves on believing the adopter was contacted. The text is only stored in a database column nobody outside the org can see. This is the single most damaging dead end in the system: it converts staff diligence into applicant ghosting. The mailto: link on the email address is the only real channel, and nothing pushes staff toward it.

top_findings.title: The website's adoption funnel is broken: the application page is orphaned and the animal modal routes to the generic contact form
top_findings.evidence: animals.html openModal() CTA (line ~336) links to contact.html?subject=adopt&about={name}, never to adopt-apply.html. Grep shows adopt-apply.html is linked only from ways-to-help.html, contact.html, names.html, and foster-apply.html — not from index.html, animals.html, or the primary nav (index.html nav-links block has no Adopt entry; Ways to Help hides inside the 'Our Story' dropdown).
top_findings.detail: The moment of peak emotional commitment — 'Meet Luna' → modal → 'Inquire about Luna' — dumps the visitor into a generic contact textarea, while the polished 3-step application with progress bar sits on a page most visitors will never find. Two competing paths (inquiry vs. application) produce inconsistent data, and neither carries a structured animal ID, so staff must parse which animal was meant from free text.

top_findings.title: Dual disconnected application systems in the app: the Applications workflow operates on seed data while real applicants sit in the Inbox
top_findings.evidence: SB- v2/src/components/features/admin/ApplicationsPage.vue reads useApplicationsStore() (Firestore 'applications' collection, populated via SEED_APPLICATIONS_QUICKSTART.md scripts); real website submissions arrive via stores/contacts.js into Inbox.vue as flattened messages. denyVolunteer/denyAdoption (lines 127-137) call store.deleteApplication — denial erases the record.
top_findings.detail: Staff have an approve/deny pipeline UI that no real applicant ever enters, and a real applicant stream (Inbox) with no workflow states beyond archive/delete. 'Deny' hard-deletes with no reason captured, no notification to the applicant, and no history for a person who may reapply. There is no interview/home-check/decision pipeline anywhere, which is the core operational flow of a rescue.

top_findings.title: No URL routing in SanctuaryBase: back button exits the app, nothing is deep-linkable, and refresh shows a fake animal
top_findings.evidence: No vue-router in SB- v2/package.json; navigation is ui.currentTab (stores/ui.js line 21); grep finds zero pushState/popstate/location.hash usage in src/. AnimalDetail.vue (~line 211) falls back to a hard-coded demo animal ('Luna', Domestic Shorthair) when ui.selectedAnimal is null — which is exactly what happens on any page refresh.
top_findings.detail: A vet tech who refreshes mid-shift on an animal's Meds tab lands on the dashboard; if state partially survives they see fabricated data for a cat named Luna — actively dangerous in a medical context. Staff cannot text each other 'check this animal's chart' links, cannot bookmark the feeding log, and the Android back gesture (the most-used gesture on a phone held in one hand at a barn) closes the app.

top_findings.title: Daily-care workflows are desktop-form-shaped, not barn-shaped: rounds are a lose-everything monolith and feeding has a dead button
top_findings.evidence: MorningRoundsPage.vue renders one form over every animal with a single 'Submit Rounds' button (line 28) — no per-animal save, no draft persistence; navigating away discards all checkboxes/notes. FeedingLogPage.vue line 122: the '✓ Fed' button on pending feedings has no @click handler (dead control). Feeding modal selects animals via a plain <select> (line 32) and bulk mode hard-codes amount: 'Standard' (line 191).
top_findings.detail: Rounds at a sanctuary happen animal-by-animal over an hour with gloves on and interruptions; a workflow that requires completing the entire roster in one session before anything persists guarantees data loss the first time a volunteer gets pulled away. The dead '✓ Fed' button means pending feedings can never be marked done from the list. Meanwhile volunteer.html line 249 promises SanctuaryBase 'works offline too' — but there is no service worker or offline persistence anywhere in SB- v2 (verified in public/ and src/), so the field-usability promise is marketing fiction.

top_findings.title: AnimalDetail's 16 flat tabs create severe information-architecture overload
top_findings.evidence: AnimalDetail.vue lines 192-208: Profile, Website, Health, Meds, Vaccines, Weight, Diet & Health, Records, Docs, Vet, Log, Met, Traits, Memory, SOS, Assess — 16 tabs, several with cryptic labels and overlapping scopes (Health vs Diet & Health vs Records vs Vet vs Log; Docs vs Records).
top_findings.detail: On a phone this is an endless horizontal strip of ambiguous words. A volunteer looking for 'when was she last vaccinated' has four plausible tabs to try. Medical data is fragmented across Health/Meds/Vaccines/Weight/Records/Vet/Met with no unified medical timeline.

top_findings.title: Navigation chrome is overcrowded for staff: 7 bottom-nav tabs plus a 33-item Admin Hub grid, with hub-and-spoke tap taxes
top_findings.evidence: BottomNav.vue lines 30-38: seven tabs for admin/staff (5 is the mobile ceiling before touch targets shrink below ~48px on 375px screens). AdminHub.vue lists ~33 features across 7 sections as a flat icon grid; FeedingLogPage.vue line 3 shows the pattern of '← Admin Hub' back buttons, so the daily loop of rounds→feeding→meds→EOD costs 2+ taps per transition through the hub.
top_findings.detail: The Admin Hub is a sitemap, not a workflow. Daily Care is the second section down, below Quick Access, even though it is the actual daily work. Staff doing the morning circuit bounce hub→feature→hub→feature all day. There is no 'My Day' task-sequenced view tying rounds, feedings, med runs, and assigned tasks into one flow (the Dashboard's command center is close, but it is stats-first, not task-first).

revamp_recommendations.title: Build a unified, structured application pipeline spanning both properties
revamp_recommendations.detail: Replace the flattened /api/contact funnel with typed endpoints (POST /api/applications with type adoption|foster|volunteer, full field payload, and animal_id). In SanctuaryBase, merge Inbox's real inflow with ApplicationsPage/AdoptionsPage into one pipeline module with stages (New → Reviewing → Interview scheduled → Home check → Approved → Adopted / Declined-with-reason), linked to the animal record and to a person record in the People CRM. Never delete on deny — capture reason and keep history for reapplicants. This single module fixes the phantom-data ApplicationsPage, the lossy contact funnel, and the missing adoption workflow at once.

revamp_recommendations.title: Add a real outbound communications layer so replies and status changes actually reach people
revamp_recommendations.detail: Wire email sending (Cloudflare Email Service or Resend) into the worker/app functions with templates: application received, interview invite, approval, denial, donation thanks. Make Inbox's 'Send reply' genuinely send, log every message to the person's timeline (donors/contacts stores), and add automatic acknowledgment emails on form submission. Until this exists, every other CRM ambition is theater — the org literally cannot close the loop with an adopter from inside the system.

revamp_recommendations.title: Redesign field workflows mobile-first with per-item saves and true offline support
revamp_recommendations.detail: Rebuild Morning Rounds as a resumable per-animal checklist (tap animal → big Fed/Water/Meds/Health toggles → auto-saves that animal → next), with progress persisted so interruptions cost nothing. Replace the feeding modal's <select> with a searchable tap-grid of animal cards; fix the dead '✓ Fed' button. Add a service worker + Firestore offline persistence with a sync queue so the 'works offline' promise on volunteer.html becomes true — spotty barn signal is the defining constraint for this user base.

revamp_recommendations.title: Introduce URL routing (vue-router) and kill the demo-data fallback
revamp_recommendations.detail: Map every tab in App.vue's routeMap to a real route (/animals/:id/meds, /admin/feeding, etc.) so back-button, refresh, bookmarks, and shareable deep links work. Remove AnimalDetail.vue's hard-coded 'Luna' fallback — on missing state, fetch by route param or show a not-found state, never fabricated medical data. This is a mostly mechanical migration given routeMap already exists as a single object.

revamp_recommendations.title: Fix the website adoption funnel: carry the animal from card to application
revamp_recommendations.detail: Change the animals.html modal CTA to 'Apply to adopt {name}' linking adopt-apply.html?animal={id} with the animal prefilled and shown as a card atop the form; keep 'Ask a question' as the secondary contact path. Add Adopt/Foster to the primary nav (adopt-apply.html is currently orphaned behind the Our Story dropdown). Relax step-3 friction: 'Anything else we should know?' should not be a required field.

revamp_recommendations.title: Give staff a task-first 'My Day' home and slim the navigation
revamp_recommendations.detail: Evolve the Dashboard's command center into a sequenced daily view: rounds due, feedings remaining (per animal, tappable), med runs, my assigned tasks, EOD report prompt after 4pm — so the daily loop happens in one place instead of hub-and-spoke through AdminHub's 33-item grid. Cut BottomNav to 5 tabs (Home, Animals, Inbox, Shifts, More) and move Vet Hub/People/Admin into a More sheet; add a search-everything command bar for the long tail.

revamp_recommendations.title: Consolidate AnimalDetail's 16 tabs into ~6 grouped sections with a unified medical timeline
revamp_recommendations.detail: Group into Profile, Medical (Health+Meds+Vaccines+Weight+Records+Vet+Met as a single chronological timeline with filters), Daily Care (Diet, feeding history, rounds notes), Behavior (Traits+Assess+SOS), Story (Memory+Docs+photos), and Website (listing). Rename cryptic labels — 'Met' and 'SOS' mean nothing to a new volunteer.

revamp_recommendations.title: Close the donor loop: pull Zeffy donations and newsletter signups into the CRM
revamp_recommendations.detail: Zeffy webhooks (or CSV import as a first step) should create/update donor records in the donors store with gift history, powering acknowledgment emails and the FinanceStats/Ledger modules from real data. Add a public 'application status' page on the website (submission ID emailed on receipt) so adopters aren't left wondering — the emotional design of the site sets a high-touch expectation the backend should honor.

strengths: The website's emotional design is genuinely excellent and should be preserved wholesale: story-led copy ('No office. No paid staff. Just people who show up for the animals.'), impact-anchored donation tiers ($25 = a week of goat feed), warm resident-animal framing on animals.html ('They're not looking for a new home — but they are why we need yours'), and the expectation-setting adoption confirmation message.

Website craft details are strong: keyboard-activatable animal cards with focus management (app.js lines 117-124, 541+), aria-live toast region, share buttons with Web Share API fallback, honeypot spam field, multi-step form with progress bar, skeleton loaders, and a modal with prev/next animal browsing.

SanctuaryBase's role-based navigation model (admin/staff/volunteer tab sets in BottomNav.vue, adminOnly filtering in AdminHub.vue) is the right architecture for a mixed staff/volunteer org and just needs pruning, not rethinking.
The Dashboard command center (Fed Today x/y progress, compliance overdue alerts, inbox notification banner) is well-conceived glanceable ops design — the seed of the 'My Day' view already exists.
The Inbox is the most polished workflow in the app: unread-since-last-open logic, filter tabs with counts, inline reply composer, two-tap armed delete, and clear type pills parsed from subject.

The website-to-app plumbing pattern is sound and secure: the functions/api/site proxy with Firebase-auth-gated server-side token forwarding, WebsiteListingTab for managing public listings from the animal record, and auction item management — the pipes exist; they just need to carry structured data.
A consistent design system (DESIGN_SYSTEM.md, CSS tokens, shared ui/ component kit, dark mode) keeps the app visually coherent across 40+ pages built quickly.

expert: JW Willeck — designer specializing in small animal-rescue web presences (Southwest Metro Animal Rescue redesign); reviewing visual design system coherence, brand consistency, and maintainability across the Saint Francis public website and SanctuaryBase v2
integration_assessment: The data plumbing between the two properties is better than most small rescues ever build: the website's _worker.js exposes a clean public API (/api/animals, /animals/featured, /stats, /contact, /newsletter) consumed by app.js, admin duties were correctly consolidated (admin.html is now a redirect card pointing to SanctuaryBase), and the app manages website content through a server-side proxy (functions/api/site/[[path]].js forwards to sfr-rescue.pages.dev/api with the site's admin token kept off the browser). The AnimalDetail "WebsiteListingTab.vue" is a genuinely great editorial workflow — publish/unpublish, AI-written public bios, cover photo, featured flags. But visually the two products share nothing: the website is a warm, editorial, light "desert sanctuary" (Fraunces serif, pine green #1f3a2e, bone paper #f4efe6, antique gold, clay, paper-grain texture), while the app is a dark neon-purple product (#1A1033 backgrounds, orange #FF7A45, glowing mint #4EFFC5, Nunito/Fredoka One, glow shadows). A volunteer moving from saintfrancisrescue.org to SanctuaryBase experiences two different organizations. Functionally, the biggest gap is applications: adopt-apply.html and volunteer-signup.html submit through generic /api/contact (SF.sendContact), landing in the site's contact inbox, while the app's ApplicationsPage.vue reads a Firestore 'applications' collection populated only by seed scripts (seed:applications) — real applications never reach the Applications module. Donations similarly bounce out to Zeffy with no return path into the donors/ledger stores.

top_findings.title: Website and app are two unrelated brands
top_findings.evidence: SaintFrancis-Website/styles.css :root (lines 50–81: --pine #1f3a2e, --bone #f4efe6, --gold, --clay; Fraunces/Hanken Grotesk, light, paper-grain body::before) vs SB- v2/src/design-tokens.css (lines 13–102: --color-bg-1 #1A1033 dark purple, --color-brand #FF7A45 orange, --color-mint #4EFFC5 neon, Nunito/'Fredoka One'). Header.vue line 10 renders 'Saint Francis Rescue' in neon mint on dark purple.
top_findings.detail: Zero shared tokens, fonts, colors, radii, or components. The website reads as an established sanctuary; the app reads as a gaming dashboard. Staff, volunteers, fosters, and board members cross between them daily (admin.html literally redirects into the app), so the brand break is felt constantly and undermines the 'one organization, one system' goal.

top_findings.title: The app's own token migration never happened — the design system documents one vocabulary while the entire codebase uses another
top_findings.evidence: DESIGN_SYSTEM.md 'Migration Guide' mandates --color-* names; grep across src/components shows ~100 of 109 .vue files using legacy shorthands (--mint, --ink, --coral, --surface, --bg) that only exist as compatibility mappings in src/style.css lines 13–59. The ui/ library itself (AppBadge.vue, AppInput.vue, AppSelect.vue, AlertBox.vue, StatCard.vue, UserRow.vue, SectionLabel.vue) uses legacy names.
top_findings.detail: Because the canonical library components use the legacy tokens, every new page copies them, so the documented system is fiction. Two names for every color doubles the vocabulary a small team must learn and makes a future re-theme (e.g., aligning to Saint Francis brand) riskier than it should be.

top_findings.title: UI-library rules are broadly violated in feature pages
top_findings.evidence: src/components/ui/README.md rules 4 and 8 ('Use <AppButton> for all buttons — never write <button class=...>', 'No hardcoded hex colors'); grep shows raw <button in 57 of ~70 feature .vue files, 29 files containing hardcoded hex (158 occurrences), and inline style attributes even inside flagship components (WebsiteListingTab.vue lines 31, 42: style="margin-top:12px").
top_findings.detail: The library exists and is good (AppButton, AppCard, AppInput, EmptyState, StatCard, PageContainer used in 49+ files), but with 40+ admin pages each hand-rolling tables, modals, tab bars, and buttons, drift compounds. The library is missing the components those pages actually need (data table, modal, page header, tab bar), which is why authors go ad-hoc.

top_findings.title: Three styling systems coexist in the app
top_findings.evidence: package.json devDependencies include tailwindcss ^4.3.1 and src/style.css line 1 is @import "tailwindcss"; ARCHITECTURE.md claims 'Tailwind CSS + CSS Variables'; yet components are styled almost entirely via scoped CSS + custom properties, with Tailwind-ish utility classes appearing in only a handful of files (LoginScreen.vue, ApplicationsPage.vue, DonorsPage.vue).
top_findings.detail: Tailwind v4, a custom token system, and per-component scoped CSS all ship together. For a small-org team this triples the ways any given style can be expressed and bloats the toolchain for near-zero payoff.

top_findings.title: Applications submitted on the website never reach the app's Applications module
top_findings.evidence: foster-apply.html line 477 and volunteer-signup.html line 835 submit via SF.sendContact / fetch('/api/contact'); _worker.js exposes no /api/applications route (route list: /animals, /contact, /newsletter, /suggestions, /admin/*); SB- v2 src/stores/applications.js reads Firestore collection 'applications', populated only by src/scripts/seedApplications.js.
top_findings.detail: The org's flagship front-door flow (adopt/foster/volunteer) degrades into unstructured contact-inbox messages, while ApplicationsPage.vue's approve/deny pipeline operates on seed data. The carefully designed multi-step form on adopt-apply.html (progress bar, quick-interest prefill) deserves a structured landing spot.

top_findings.title: Theme persistence is split across three writers with mismatched localStorage keys
top_findings.evidence: SB- v2/index.html flash-prevention script reads 'sb_theme'; src/App.vue lines 146–150 read/write 'sb_theme'; src/stores/ui.js line 32 toggleTheme writes 'theme' (dead key).
top_findings.detail: A theme toggled through ui.toggleTheme persists to a key nothing reads, so the choice can silently fail to survive reload depending on which code path runs. Symptomatic of the broader pattern: system rules defined in one place, re-implemented differently elsewhere.

top_findings.title: App typography undermines accessibility and diverges from the website's font strategy
top_findings.evidence: SB- v2/design-tokens.css lines 105–114: px-based scale with --font-size-base: 13px; src/style.css line 3 imports Google Fonts via render-blocking CSS @import. Website styles.css lines 1–43 self-hosts Fraunces/Hanken woff2 with a comment 'was Google Fonts; now first-party for speed + privacy'.
top_findings.detail: 13px px-locked body text ignores user font-size preferences and is small for volunteers using the app in barns on phones; the website already solved the font-hosting question correctly and the app ignores that precedent. 'Fredoka One' also gives the app a playful-toddler display face that clashes with the website's dignified Fraunces.

top_findings.title: Documentation drift between DESIGN_SYSTEM.md / ARCHITECTURE.md and reality
top_findings.evidence: DESIGN_SYSTEM.md shows AppCard variants ('elevated', 'outlined', 'gradient') and alert classes (.success-box/.warn-box) alongside the differently-specified ui/README.md (AlertBox component, different AppButton variant list omitting 'ghost' which exists in AppButton.vue line 15); ARCHITECTURE.md folder listing omits ~20 real stores (quarantine, waivers, safeHouses, auctionItems, animalsAdmin).
top_findings.detail: For a small org where the docs ARE the design team, three partially contradictory sources of truth (DESIGN_SYSTEM.md, ui/README.md, actual components) guarantee continued drift. One of them must win.

revamp_recommendations.title: One brand, one token source: re-skin SanctuaryBase in the Saint Francis identity
revamp_recommendations.detail: Extract the website's palette and type into a shared brand token layer (pine/moss/sage greens, bone/cream surfaces, gold/clay accents, Fraunces for display, Hanken Grotesk for UI) published as a single design-tokens file consumed by both styles.css and the app's design-tokens.css. Make light 'sanctuary paper' the app's default theme and rebuild dark mode as a deep-pine night theme (useful for evening rounds) instead of the current purple/neon-mint scheme. Keep the app's excellent token architecture (semantic scales, faint/glow variants, light-mode overrides in design-tokens.css lines 228–317) — only the values change, which the compatibility-mapping layer in style.css actually makes feasible in one pass.

revamp_recommendations.title: Finish the token migration and enforce it mechanically
revamp_recommendations.detail: Codemod all legacy names (--mint, --ink, --coral, --surface, --bg, --teal-*) to the canonical --color-* names starting with the ui/ library itself, then delete the shim block in src/style.css lines 13–59. Add stylelint with two rules — no raw hex in .vue files, no unknown custom properties — wired into the build. Fix the theme key mismatch by making ui.js the single theme owner using 'sb_theme'. This is a week of work that permanently ends the two-vocabulary problem.

revamp_recommendations.title: Expand the ui/ library to cover what the 40+ admin pages actually build by hand
revamp_recommendations.detail: Add the missing primitives that grep shows every admin page reinventing: AppTable (sortable/filterable rows — PeopleListTable.vue, LedgerPage.vue, WaiversPage.vue each have their own), AppModal (replacing per-page overlay divs like GrantDetailModal.vue, DietModal.vue, ConfirmDeleteDialog.vue), PageHeader (title + actions row duplicated in ~50 files), AppTabs (AnimalDetail.vue's 11 tabs and VetHub sub-tabs hand-roll the same pattern), and FormField/AppTextarea/AppDatePicker. Then collapse DESIGN_SYSTEM.md and ui/README.md into one document that matches the code — for a two-or-three-maintainer org, the README-in-the-components-folder is the right single source of truth.

revamp_recommendations.title: Drop Tailwind; standardize on tokens + scoped CSS + the ui/ library
revamp_recommendations.detail: Tailwind v4 is imported (style.css line 1) but used in only a handful of files; remove @import 'tailwindcss', the tailwindcss/@tailwindcss/postcss/autoprefixer devDependencies, and convert the few utility-class stragglers (LoginScreen.vue, ApplicationsPage.vue, DonorsPage.vue) to the house pattern. One styling system a small team fully understands beats two systems half-used.

revamp_recommendations.title: Structured application intake: make the website's forms feed the Applications module
revamp_recommendations.detail: Add POST /api/applications to the website _worker.js (type: adoption|foster|volunteer, animal_id, structured answers from the existing multi-step forms in adopt-apply.html/foster-apply.html/volunteer-signup.html), and have SanctuaryBase's applications store read real submissions through the existing /api/site proxy (the same pattern contactsService.js already proves out) instead of the seeded Firestore collection. Add status-change touchpoints (received/reviewing/approved emails) so the public site and internal pipeline are one funnel. This single module makes the website a true front door rather than a brochure with a mailbox.

revamp_recommendations.title: Close the donation loop into donors and ledger
revamp_recommendations.detail: Donations currently exit to Zeffy (donate.html) and vanish from the system's view. Add a Zeffy webhook/CSV-import endpoint feeding the donors.js and ledger.js stores so FinanceStatsPage and DonorsPage reflect real giving, and surface a 'thanks + impact' page on the website driven by the same /api/stats the homepage already consumes. Pairs naturally with the existing newsletter subscriber flow.

revamp_recommendations.title: Brand kit for app-generated public artifacts
revamp_recommendations.detail: GrantLetterPage.vue, NewsletterPage.vue, and future adoption certificates produce documents that represent the organization externally; template them with the website's Fraunces/pine/gold identity (shared logo asset, letterhead partial) so everything a donor or grantor receives matches saintfrancisrescue.org. Currently the app's Header.vue falls back to hiding a missing /logo.png — the two properties don't even share a logo file.

revamp_recommendations.title: App typography and accessibility pass
revamp_recommendations.detail: Self-host the app's fonts as woff2 like the website does (styles.css lines 1–43 is the template), replace the render-blocking Google Fonts @import in style.css line 3, convert the px font scale in design-tokens.css to rem with a larger effective base (~15–16px) for barn/phone use, and retire 'Fredoka One' in favor of the shared display face. The app's existing prefers-reduced-motion and prefers-contrast handling is solid — keep it and add it to the website, which currently lacks the reduced-motion guard for its reveal animations.

strengths: The website's design system is genuinely excellent for a small nonprofit: one coherent token set in styles.css (pine/bone/gold/clay, Fraunces/Hanken), self-hosted fonts with a documented rationale, tasteful signature details (paper-grain body::before, .eyebrow with gold rule, .divider-gold), and consistent button/card vocabulary across ~20 static pages — preserve this identity as the org brand.

The app has a real component library with teeth: src/components/ui with a barrel export, a README that states usage rules explicitly, and strong core components (AppButton with variants/sizes/loading state, AppCard with header/footer slots, EmptyState, StatCard, PageContainer used in 49+ files).

The token architecture in design-tokens.css is professionally structured — semantic color families with light/dark/faint/glow variants, full light-mode override block, z-index scale, motion tokens, prefers-reduced-motion and prefers-contrast support — only its values and adoption need work, not its design.

The integration plumbing is the right shape: public read API on the website worker, server-side authenticated proxy (functions/api/site/[[path]].js) keeping the site admin token out of the browser, admin consolidation via admin.html redirect, and the WebsiteListingTab publish/unpublish workflow with AI bio generation — a model for how the remaining modules (applications, donations) should connect.

The website's front-end craft is high: accessible toast/live-region pattern in app.js, keyboard activation for animal cards, analytics that 'must never break the page', escaped HTML rendering helpers, and noscript fallbacks for reveal animations.

expert: Nonprofit SEO & Content Strategy Lead (INSIDEA-style agency review)
integration_assessment: The integration is real but shallow, and it runs in the wrong direction for SEO. SanctuaryBase (Vue/Firebase, C:/Users/lawye/Projects/SB- v2) is the system of record for animals (Firestore `animals` + `applications` collections), and staff manually "publish" an animal to the website via WebsiteListingTab.vue → /api/site proxy (functions/api/site/[[path]].js, src/services/siteApi.js) → the website Worker's /api/admin/animals routes, which write to a completely separate Neon Postgres `animals` table (_worker.js ~line 14555). So public animal content is a hand-synced duplicate keyed by `siteAnimalId`. Inbound, the website's adoption/foster forms (adopt-apply.html line 464+) flatten a rich 11-field application into a single text blob POSTed to /api/contact → Neon `contacts` table → surfaced in SanctuaryBase's Inbox — while SanctuaryBase's dedicated `applications` Firestore store (src/stores/applications.js) never receives them; two disjoint pipelines. Impact stats are mostly hard-coded in the Worker (/api/stats returns yearsActive:14, animalsRescued:300, volunteers:22 as literals), so the rich operational data in SB (adoptions, outcomes, financeStats, feedingLogs) never feeds public storytelling or SEO content. Donations bypass both systems entirely (Zeffy external links), so donor data never reaches SB's donors store from the web. For SEO purposes, the app is a content goldmine (animal bios with AI writer, adoption outcomes, medical journeys, events/auction items) that currently emits only a thin, client-side-rendered JSON feed with zero crawlable URLs per animal.

top_findings.title: No indexable per-animal pages — the site's highest-value SEO content is invisible
top_findings.evidence: animals.html line 113 renders an empty #animals-grid filled client-side by app.js (SF.getAnimals → /api/animals JSON); 'detail' views are hash URLs (app.js line 385: /animals.html#animal-{id}). _worker.js line 15098 only handles /api/* and /media/* — no HTML route per animal.
top_findings.detail: Queries like 'adopt [breed] Yuma', '[species] rescue Arizona', or an animal's name have no landing page. Hash fragments are not separate URLs to Google; the grid depends on JS rendering of an API call; there is no Product/ItemList/per-animal schema, no unique title/meta/OG per animal, and shared animal links all preview as the generic opengraph.jpg. This is the single largest organic-traffic loss for an adoption org — per-animal pages are what Petfinder/Adopt-a-Pet rank with.

top_findings.title: Canonical domain is sfr-rescue.pages.dev — all equity accrues to a Cloudflare subdomain
top_findings.evidence: Every canonical, og:url, schema url, sitemap.xml <loc>, and robots.txt Sitemap line points to https://sfr-rescue.pages.dev (e.g., index.html line 8). Meanwhile donate.html Zeffy links carry utm_source=saintfrancisrescue.org and a commented Plausible block references saintfrancisrescue.org — the intended brand domain was never wired up.
top_findings.detail: pages.dev subdomains have weak trust signals, can't build brand-domain authority, look unprofessional in SERPs and donor emails, and complicate a future migration (every URL, schema block, and GSC property will need to move — the longer it waits, the more equity is lost). Buying/attaching the custom domain and 301ing pages.dev is prerequisite to everything else.

top_findings.title: SEO_IMPROVEMENTS.md materially misrepresents what is implemented
top_findings.evidence: C:/Users/lawye/Projects/SaintFrancis-Website/SEO_IMPROVEMENTS.md claims: #1 sitemap 'with 18 pages and image metadata' (sitemap.xml has 14 URLs, zero image tags); #4 'AggregateRating schema with 5-star rating and 3 reviews' (grep for AggregateRating across all HTML: zero hits); #14 'manifest.json and apple-mobile-web-app references removed' (index.html lines 33–36 still contain all of them); #6 breadcrumbs on 'index.html' (index.html has no BreadcrumbList).
top_findings.detail: The doc is the team's SEO source of truth and it can't be trusted. The one silver lining: the fabricated 3-review AggregateRating it claims would violate Google's structured-data guidelines (self-serving reviews on LocalBusiness are banned), so its absence in code is actually correct — the doc should be rewritten as an honest done/not-done ledger.

top_findings.title: Adoption/foster applications are unstructured contact blobs that never reach the app's applications module
top_findings.evidence: adopt-apply.html lines 464–500 concatenate 11 structured fields (interest, experience, setup, other-animals, care, goals...) into SF.sendContact → POST /api/contact → Neon contactsTable (_worker.js line 14655). SB's src/stores/applications.js reads a Firestore 'applications' collection that nothing on the website writes to.
top_findings.detail: Beyond the ops pain, this kills conversion measurement and content strategy: you can't tie an application to a specific animal listing (no animal ID field), can't compute page→application conversion by species/breed to guide content investment, and can't publish 'X applications this month' style social proof.

top_findings.title: Sitemap is stale, incomplete, and static while the animal inventory changes daily
top_findings.evidence: sitemap.xml: 14 URLs, lastmod values of 2026-06-06/2026-07-01, missing names.html, privacy, terms, accessibility, and (necessarily) any animal URLs; animals entry declares changefreq=daily but the file is hand-edited. robots.txt is 4 lines with no Disallow for admin.html or /api/.
top_findings.detail: A Worker route (the Hono app is already there) should generate sitemap.xml dynamically from the animals table with real lastmod dates, and robots.txt should disallow /admin and /api/ paths (admin.html is currently crawlable and indexable).

top_findings.title: Impact stats are hard-coded lies-in-waiting instead of live data from SanctuaryBase
top_findings.evidence: _worker.js line 14819: /api/stats returns yearsActive:14, animalsRescued:300, volunteers:22 as literals; index.html line 96 hard-codes 'Hundreds'. SB v2 has adoptions.js, outcomes.js, financeStats.js, feedingLogs.js stores full of real numbers.
top_findings.detail: Fresh, specific impact numbers ('87 animals adopted in 2026', '312,000 lbs of feed served') are E-E-A-T and conversion gold, and Google increasingly rewards freshness on nonprofit money pages. Static numbers rot and erode trust.

top_findings.title: Schema quality bugs: malformed sameAs and a self-canonicalized 404 page
top_findings.evidence: animals.html line 35 schema includes "donate.html" (a relative page link) inside the sameAs array; 404.html line 9 sets rel=canonical to https://sfr-rescue.pages.dev/404.html.
top_findings.detail: sameAs must be absolute URLs of official profiles — a relative 'donate.html' fails validation and dilutes the entity signal. A 404 page should return 404 status with no canonical; canonicalizing it invites indexing of the error page.

top_findings.title: Zero editorial/content engine: no blog, success stories, or events pages despite abundant source material
top_findings.evidence: Site inventory (17 HTML pages) contains no /news, /stories, /events, or per-campaign pages; press.html is a static media kit; fundraiser.html is one page fed by auctionItemsTable. SB v2 holds outcomes.js, adoptions.js, announcements.js, auctionItems.js — all unpublished narrative material.
top_findings.detail: Rescue orgs win long-tail SEO with story content ('senior horse rehabilitation', 'how to foster a goat in Arizona') and Event schema for fundraisers. The site currently has nothing for Google to index between the 14 static pages — no freshness signal, no long-tail surface, no internal-linking targets.

revamp_recommendations.title: Server-render per-animal pages from the Worker with full schema and OG tags
revamp_recommendations.detail: The Hono app in _worker.js already owns routing and the Neon animals table — add a GET /animals/:id-:slug HTML route that server-renders the animal's profile (name, species, breed, age, story, photos) with unique <title>/meta/canonical/og:image (their actual photo, not opengraph.jpg) and JSON-LD (ItemPage + an offer-style listing; Google has no Animal type, but Product-like or CreativeWork markup plus BreadcrumbList works, and image/description feed image search). Keep the JS grid as the browse UI linking to real URLs instead of #animal-N hashes. This turns every rescue into a landing page and every adoption share into a rich social card. SanctuaryBase's WebsiteListingTab already controls publish state — no new editorial workflow needed.

revamp_recommendations.title: Move to the brand custom domain and 301 pages.dev
revamp_recommendations.detail: Attach saintfrancisrescue.org (already referenced in the site's own UTM params) to the Cloudflare Pages project, bulk-update canonicals/og:url/schema/sitemap/robots (18 files, one find-replace), 301 sfr-rescue.pages.dev, re-verify in Google Search Console, and update Plausible data-domain. Do this before any content investment so new equity lands on the real domain.

revamp_recommendations.title: Generate sitemap.xml and robots.txt dynamically from the database
revamp_recommendations.detail: Add Worker routes for /sitemap.xml (static pages + every published animal URL with real lastmod from createdAt/updatedAt, plus image:image tags for animal photos) and /robots.txt (Disallow: /admin, /api/). This makes the daily-changing animal inventory self-indexing and ends hand-maintained sitemap drift. Ping GSC on publish from the SB WebsiteListingTab flow.

revamp_recommendations.title: New module: Success Stories / News publisher inside SanctuaryBase
revamp_recommendations.detail: Add a 'Stories' feature in SB (new Pinia store + editor page, mirroring the existing WebsiteListingTab publish pattern) that pushes posts to a new Neon `stories` table, with the Worker server-rendering /stories/:slug pages (Article schema, author=NGO, datePublished). Seed it from adoption outcomes (outcomes.js/adoptions.js) — 'From quarantine to couch: Bella's 8-month journey' — and reuse the existing 'Write with AI' bio generator (functions/api/generate-animal-bio.js) as a story-draft assistant. This is the freshness/long-tail engine the site completely lacks, and it internal-links to animal and donate pages.

revamp_recommendations.title: Structured application pipeline: website forms → Firestore applications collection
revamp_recommendations.detail: Replace the flatten-to-contact hack: give the Worker a POST /api/applications endpoint that stores structured fields including an optional animalId (pre-filled when applying from a per-animal page), and have SB's applications store ingest them (either the site proxy pulls, or the Worker forwards to a Cloudflare function writing to Firestore via Admin SDK). Adds conversion tracking (SF.track already exists) so you can measure listing-page → application rate per species and steer content spend.

revamp_recommendations.title: Live impact stats API fed by SanctuaryBase data
revamp_recommendations.detail: Replace the hard-coded /api/stats literals with numbers synced from SB (a scheduled function aggregating adoptions/outcomes/financeStats into a stats table or KV). Surface them on index.html (already wired via data-stat attributes) and in a new /impact page with year-by-year numbers — prime material for grant applications, press, and 'animal rescue statistics Yuma' queries. Add matching schema (e.g., NGO with quantified description) and keep numberOfEmployees:0 which is a great trust signal.

revamp_recommendations.title: Event schema and event pages for fundraisers/auctions
revamp_recommendations.detail: auctionItemsTable already has endsAt and the SB AuctionItemsPage manages items — add per-event pages (or enrich fundraiser.html) with schema.org/Event markup (name, startDate/endDate, location, organizer NGO) so fundraisers appear in Google's event surfaces and local 'things to do Yuma' queries. Publish upcoming events from SB's announcements store.

revamp_recommendations.title: Schema and hygiene fix pass + rewrite SEO_IMPROVEMENTS.md as an honest ledger
revamp_recommendations.detail: Fix animals.html sameAs (remove 'donate.html'), drop the canonical from 404.html, add BreadcrumbList to index.html or delete the claim, remove or implement the manifest cleanup, add og:locale, and rewrite SEO_IMPROVEMENTS.md to reflect only what ships (explicitly noting AggregateRating was intentionally NOT added because self-serving review markup violates Google guidelines). Add a Google Business Profile task — the doc mentions 'if created', and for local pack visibility it matters more than half the schema on the site.

strengths: Genuinely strong on-page fundamentals across all 17 pages: unique keyword+location titles, 155-char meta descriptions, canonicals, full OG/Twitter card sets, and consistent NGO/AnimalShelter JSON-LD with taxID, nonprofitStatus, PO Box address, and sameAs social profiles (index.html line 31 is a model NGO schema block).
Valid FAQPage schema on faq.html (line 148) built from real on-page Q&A, and BreadcrumbList on nearly every subpage — legitimate rich-result eligibility.
DonateAction schema with EntryPoint and PriceSpecification on donate.html (line 104) — rare to see done at all, let alone correctly, on small nonprofit sites.
Excellent, story-driven copywriting with descriptive image alt text ('Kharma and her daughter Victoria, two rescued horses...') and strong local signals (Yuma, AZ woven naturally throughout).
Performance and accessibility foundations that protect Core Web Vitals: font/hero preloads with fetchpriority, webp images, skip links, noscript reveal fallback, semantic heading structure.

A working, security-conscious publish pipeline already exists between SanctuaryBase and the website (WebsiteListingTab.vue → /api/site Firebase-verified proxy → Worker admin routes, token never in browser) — the plumbing for dynamic SEO content is 70% built; it just needs crawlable output on the other end.
The AI bio writer (generate-animal-bio.js + 'Write with AI' in WebsiteListingTab) is an editorial force multiplier for a volunteer-run org with no content staff.
Privacy-friendly Plausible analytics with donate-click event tracking (SF.track in app.js) — conversion measurement scaffolding is already in place.

expert: Whole Whale-style nonprofit analytics and digital strategy consultant — measurement, conversion tracking, and KPI/data strategy review
integration_assessment: The two systems share operational data but almost no measurement data. The website runs Plausible (index.html:25, data-domain "sfr-rescue.pages.dev") plus a well-built custom event layer (SF.track in app.js:100-115) that fires "Donate Click", "Newsletter Signup", "Sticky Bar Shown/Dismissed", "Exit Intent Shown", "Animal Shared", and "Name Suggested". SanctuaryBase proxies into the site's Worker API server-side (SB functions/api/site/[[path]].js with SITE_ADMIN_TOKEN) to read/reply to contacts, view newsletter subscribers, and manage public animal/auction listings — a solid pattern. But the measurement chain breaks at every conversion point: (1) donations happen entirely off-site on Zeffy — the site only tracks click intent, and no Zeffy revenue ever flows into SanctuaryBase's ledger, financeStats, or donors collections; (2) adoption/foster/volunteer applications submit as generic contact-form rows (adopt-apply.html:495 → SF.sendContact → _worker.js POST /contact into a contacts table), while the app's ApplicationsPage reads a completely separate Firestore 'applications' collection that website submissions never touch — the pipeline UI is effectively decorative; (3) zero analytics events fire on successful application submission (grep confirms 0 SF.track calls in adopt-apply, foster-apply, volunteer-signup); (4) the public /stats endpoint hardcodes yearsActive:14, animalsRescued:300, volunteers:22 (_worker.js:14819-14832) even though the app has real volunteer and outcome data. The org can see traffic and clicks but is flying blind on revenue, donor retention, application funnels, cost per animal, and true impact numbers.

top_findings.title: Donation revenue is a measurement black hole
top_findings.evidence: donate.html:156-670 (all CTAs link to zeffy.com with UTM params); app.js:107-115 tracks only 'Donate Click' intent and sets a sessionStorage flag that is never read for conversion matching; no Zeffy webhook/import anywhere in either codebase; SB- v2 ledger.js and donors.js have no external ingestion
top_findings.detail: Every donation path exits to Zeffy in a new tab. The site tracks the click but never the completion, so conversion rate, average gift, recurring-vs-one-time mix, and campaign ROI are all unknowable. Zeffy revenue also never reaches SanctuaryBase — the ledger, financeStats, and donor records are populated only by manual entry, so the org's actual income is invisible to its own management system.

top_findings.title: Website applications never enter the app's application pipeline
top_findings.evidence: adopt-apply.html:495 (SF.sendContact with subject:'adopt'), foster-apply.html and volunteer-signup.html:835 (fetch('/api/contact')); _worker.js:14655 stores these in the contacts table; SB- v2 src/stores/applications.js reads Firestore 'applications' collection; ApplicationsPage.vue:107-117 filters that Firestore data — website submissions never reach it
top_findings.detail: Structured multi-step application data (experience, setup, other animals, goals) is flattened into a generic contact message. The app's ApplicationsPage with Approve/Deny workflow operates on a disconnected Firestore collection that real applicants never populate. Result: no application funnel exists — submitted→reviewed→approved→adopted conversion rates, time-to-decision, and drop-off are unmeasurable, and staff triage applications as inbox messages.

top_findings.title: Zero conversion events on the highest-value forms, and stale Plausible config
top_findings.evidence: grep count of SF.track: adopt-apply.html=0, foster-apply.html=0, volunteer-signup.html=0; successful-submit handlers fire no analytics event; index.html:25 data-domain='sfr-rescue.pages.dev' while canonical URLs moved to the live domain (commit ad897ac); SB App.vue:184-185 sends userId to Plausible
top_findings.detail: The custom event layer exists and works (Newsletter Signup fires at app.js:761), but adoption, foster, and volunteer application submissions — the site's primary conversions besides donations — emit no event, and multi-step form abandonment is untracked. Meanwhile the Plausible data-domain still points at the pages.dev host, so traffic on the production custom domain may be undercounted or split, and the internal app sends user identifiers as event props.

top_findings.title: Two disconnected finance systems with seeded/derived metrics that are wrong
top_findings.evidence: SB- v2 src/stores/financeStats.js (collection 'financeStats', seeded via FINANCE_STATS_SETUP.md sample-data scripts) vs src/stores/ledger.js (collection 'ledgers'); FinanceStatsPage.vue:101-107 computes 'Active Donors' as unique description strings among only the 10 most recent transactions (financeStats.js:57-68 slices to 10)
top_findings.detail: Finance data lives in two parallel Firestore collections with no reconciliation, and the Finance Stats dashboard was bootstrapped from seed data. The 'Active Donors' KPI is derived from transaction description text of the last 10 rows — a meaningless number presented as a headline metric. Any board report built from this page would be wrong.

top_findings.title: Donor CRM cannot answer a single retention question
top_findings.evidence: SB- v2 src/stores/donors.js is flat CRUD with no gift subcollection or aggregation; DonorsPage.vue:7 hardcodes StatCard 'This Year' to "0"; 'Send Thank You' and 'View History' buttons (DonorsPage.vue:66-69) have no click handlers; donor records reference totalDonations/lastDonation fields nothing computes
top_findings.detail: There is no gift-history model, so donor retention rate, LYBUNT/SYBUNT, upgrade/downgrade, and recurring-donor counts are structurally impossible. Donations are logged as donor attributes at entry time, disconnected from the ledger, and the acknowledgment workflow is dead UI.

top_findings.title: Denying an application deletes the record, destroying funnel history
top_findings.evidence: ApplicationsPage.vue:127-137 — denyVolunteer and denyAdoption both call store.deleteApplication(id)
top_findings.detail: Approval rate, denial reasons, and repeat-applicant detection are unmeasurable because the deny path is a hard delete. Outcomes should be status transitions ('denied' + reason + timestamp), never deletions.

top_findings.title: Public impact numbers are hardcoded fiction while real data exists in the app
top_findings.evidence: _worker.js:14819-14832 GET /stats returns yearsActive:14, animalsRescued:300, volunteers:22 as literals, and 'adoptedThisYear' filters on animal createdAt year rather than adoption date; SB- v2 has real volunteer counts (Dashboard.vue:211), clock-in hours (VolunteerHoursPage.vue), and outcomes (outcomes.js)
top_findings.detail: The homepage impact strip is powered by constants, and the one live query is wrong for any animal adopted in a later year than intake. Donors are shown numbers no one can stand behind.

top_findings.title: Outcome/LRR metrics are copy-pasted shelter boilerplate that doesn't fit the org
top_findings.evidence: OutcomesPage.vue:127-129 computes dogsLRR and catsLRR for a farm-animal/reptile sanctuary (donate.html:103 schema.org describes a 'farm animal rescue and sanctuary'); outcomes store is separate from animals status transitions with no automatic linkage
top_findings.detail: Live Release Rate hardcodes Dogs and Cats species splits irrelevant to this sanctuary's population, and outcomes are manually entered rather than derived from animal status changes — length-of-stay and adoption-rate metrics can silently diverge from animal records.

revamp_recommendations.title: Close the donation loop: Zeffy ingestion into ledger + donors
revamp_recommendations.detail: Add a donation-ingestion module in SanctuaryBase: a Cloudflare function receiving Zeffy webhooks (or scheduled CSV import) that writes each gift to a canonical 'gifts' collection linked to a donor record (matched by email), and rolls up into the ledger as income with source/campaign tags carried from the UTM parameters already present on every donate link. Redirect Zeffy back to a /thank-you page on the site that fires a 'Donation Completed' Plausible goal with amount, closing the click→completion funnel that app.js currently abandons at 'Donate Click'.

revamp_recommendations.title: Structured applications API feeding the app's real pipeline
revamp_recommendations.detail: Replace the SF.sendContact shim on adopt-apply/foster-apply/volunteer-signup with a POST /api/applications endpoint in _worker.js that preserves the structured fields, then sync into the Firestore 'applications' collection that ApplicationsPage already consumes (reuse the existing token-protected proxy pattern). Record status transitions (submitted/contacted/approved/denied/completed) with timestamps and reasons — and change deny from delete to status update — so approval rate, time-to-decision, and animal-level demand become reportable.

revamp_recommendations.title: Nonprofit KPI dashboard module (board-ready Reports hub)
revamp_recommendations.detail: Build a Reports hub in SanctuaryBase computing from canonical data: donor retention rate and LYBUNT/SYBUNT (from gifts), monthly recurring donor count, average gift and gifts-per-donor; cost per animal per month (ledger expenses ÷ animal-days by species using intake/outcome dates); adoption rate and average length of stay (from status transitions); volunteer hours and 90-day volunteer retention (clockIn data already powers VolunteerHoursPage); application funnel conversion; newsletter list growth by source (source field already captured). Add month-over-month trends and CSV export for board packets and grant applications.

revamp_recommendations.title: Unify finance: retire financeStats as a stored collection
revamp_recommendations.detail: Merge the parallel 'financeStats' and 'ledgers' Firestore collections into one ledger source of truth (financeStats is seeded sample data per FINANCE_STATS_SETUP.md), and rewrite FinanceStatsPage's categories/donor counts as an analytics layer over the full ledger — fixing the 'Active Donors from last 10 transaction descriptions' bug. Tag every income row with donorId, source (zeffy/auction/in-kind/grant), and campaign.

revamp_recommendations.title: Fix analytics plumbing and event taxonomy
revamp_recommendations.detail: Update the Plausible data-domain to the live production domain (still 'sfr-rescue.pages.dev' at index.html:25), define Plausible goals for Donation Completed, Application Submitted (by type), and Newsletter Signup, add SF.track calls in the success handlers of the three application forms plus step-abandonment events for the multi-step forms (step state already exists client-side). In the app, drop userId/animalName props from Plausible events.

revamp_recommendations.title: Attribution capture on every inbound record
revamp_recommendations.detail: Store utm_source/medium/campaign, referrer, and landing page on contact, application, and newsletter rows at submission time in _worker.js (the newsletter table already has a 'source' column — extend that pattern). This is the only way to answer 'which channel produces adopters and donors, not just visitors' and to justify social/ad effort.

revamp_recommendations.title: Make public impact numbers live
revamp_recommendations.detail: Replace the hardcoded /stats constants in _worker.js with real values: volunteer count and hours synced nightly from SanctuaryBase, adoptions-this-year computed from outcome dates (not animal createdAt year), and a maintained lifetime-rescues counter. Honest, moving numbers outperform static claims on donation pages.

revamp_recommendations.title: Sanctuary-appropriate outcome metrics
revamp_recommendations.detail: Rework OutcomesPage to compute LRR and length-of-stay per actual species in the population (goats, horses, reptiles — not hardcoded dogs/cats), derive outcomes automatically from animal status transitions instead of manual entry, and track permanent sanctuary residents separately from adoptable animals so adoption-rate denominators are correct.

strengths: The SF.track abstraction (app.js:100-115) is genuinely good: provider-agnostic (Plausible with gtag fallback), fail-safe try/catch, and an intentional event taxonomy (Donate Click with location+target, Sticky Bar, Exit Intent, Animal Shared) — a solid foundation to extend rather than replace.

Consistent UTM discipline: every Zeffy donate link carries utm_source/medium/campaign, with utm_content variants distinguishing exit-intent and impact-slider placements (donate.html:156,444,670) — the attribution inputs exist even though nothing consumes them yet.

The server-side proxy pattern (SB- v2 functions/api/site/[[path]].js) is well designed: Firebase role verified server-side, site admin token never exposed to the browser, role-scoped path allowances — the right chassis for deeper bidirectional data flow.

Real operational telemetry already exists in the app: clock-in-based volunteer hours with per-volunteer trends and period filters (VolunteerHoursPage.vue), fed-today coverage and compliance-overdue metrics on the Dashboard (Dashboard.vue:224-253) — honest, computed-from-source metrics worth building the KPI layer on.

Newsletter capture is properly centralized (POST /api/newsletter with dedupe and a source field, _worker.js:14676) and surfaced read-only in the app via newsletterService — a working, if minimal, website→app data flow to use as the template.

Documentation culture in SB- v2 (ARCHITECTURE.md, CODEBASE_MAP.md, setup guides) and the uniform store-per-domain Pinia pattern make the revamp tractable — a reporting layer can be added without restructuring.

expert: Intopia-style digital accessibility strategist (disability-led WCAG 2.2 AA compliance review of the public website)
integration_assessment: The two systems connect through a thin, admin-only proxy: SanctuaryBase's Cloudflare function (SB- v2/functions/api/site/[[path]].js) verifies Firebase role server-side and forwards to the website Worker's /api/admin/* routes (animals, auction items, contacts), with siteApi.js/contactsService.js on the Vue side. Public-facing flow is one-directional and lossy from an accessibility standpoint: adoption and foster applications on the website are submitted as generic contact messages (adopt-apply.html posts to SF.sendContact with subject:"adopt"), so they arrive in SanctuaryBase's contact inbox rather than its structured applications store (src/stores/applications.js), and applicants get no accessible status tracking afterward. Animal listings flow website→app for admin editing, but there is no alt-text requirement in that pipeline — the accessibility statement itself admits volunteer-uploaded photos "may occasionally be brief or generic," and the animalCard renderer (app.js:127-144) falls back to just the animal's name as alt. The website has had a genuine accessibility investment pass (skip links, live regions, focus-visible rings, reduced motion); the newer conversion-focused additions (multi-step forms, quick-interest widgets, exit-intent modal, clickable div cards) were bolted on without the same rigor and are where most WCAG failures now live.

top_findings.title: Mouse-only custom controls: clickable divs with no keyboard access (WCAG 2.1.1)
top_findings.evidence: adopt-apply.html:402 (animal grid cards rendered as <div ... onclick="alert(...)"> with no tabindex/role/keydown); app.js:444-457 (volunteer page .time-option divs get only click listeners, no tabindex or key handling); volunteer.html time-commitment selector injected by same code
top_findings.detail: The 'available for adoption' grid on the adoption application page and the volunteer time-commitment selector are completely unreachable and inoperable by keyboard and invisible as controls to screen readers. The adopt grid additionally uses alert() to convey the animal description, which is a jarring, unstructured experience. This directly blocks disabled users on the two highest-value conversion pages. Contrast with the shared animalCard() renderer (app.js:118-144) which correctly adds tabindex=0 and Enter/Space activation — the pattern exists in the codebase but was not used here.

top_findings.title: Multi-step adopt/foster forms fail error identification: the .error class has no CSS and no ARIA (WCAG 3.3.1, 4.1.3)
top_findings.evidence: adopt-apply.html:429-441 (validateStep adds/removes class 'error' only); grep of styles.css shows no .error rule exists, only .field.invalid (styles.css:986-992) which this code never sets; foster-apply.html uses the identical pattern; step counter/progress bar (adopt-apply.html:144-152) is bare divs with no role=progressbar or announcement on step change
top_findings.detail: When a user hits 'Next' with empty required fields, invalid fields receive a class that styles nothing, sets no aria-invalid, no aria-describedby, and no per-field message; the only feedback is a generic status line ('Please fill in all required fields before continuing') that is not focused. Screen-reader and low-vision users cannot locate which field failed. Step changes (display:none toggling) are also unannounced, so a screen-reader user does not know they moved from Step 1 to Step 2. The site's own contact form has a proper inline-validation engine (app.js initInlineValidation, lines 484-539, with role=alert field errors) that is wired only to #contact-form.

top_findings.title: Unassociated labels on all three 'quick interest' pre-forms (WCAG 1.3.1, 3.3.2, 4.1.2)
top_findings.evidence: adopt-apply.html:109-129 (<label style=...>What type of animal interests you?</label> followed by <input id="qi-interest"> — no for attribute, not wrapping); foster-apply.html:109-122 and volunteer-signup.html:148,160 repeat the same pattern
top_findings.detail: Every field in the quick-interest widgets on adopt-apply, foster-apply, and volunteer-signup has a visually adjacent but programmatically orphaned label. Screen readers announce these inputs and selects with no accessible name at all. Also affected: the radio groups 'Will you have other animals around?' (adopt-apply.html:195-200) and 'What's your living situation?' (foster-apply.html:173-178) use a bare <label> as a group caption with no fieldset/legend and no association, so the group question is never announced with the radio options.

top_findings.title: Monthly/one-time donation toggle has no accessible name (WCAG 4.1.2, 1.3.1)
top_findings.evidence: donate.html:224-232 — <input type="checkbox" id="monthlyToggle"> inside <label class="switch"> whose only content is an empty <span class="slider">; the 'One-time'/'Monthly' text spans are siblings outside the label
top_findings.detail: The frequency switch on the primary donation card is announced as an unnamed checkbox with no indication it controls one-time vs. monthly giving, and its state ('12x impact') is conveyed only by visual label opacity (.toggle-label.active, opacity 0.8 vs 1 — also a contrast/state-by-color-only concern). A screen-reader donor cannot tell what they are toggling on the most important form on the site. Positive nearby: the range slider has an sr-only label (donate.html:211) and the impact box is aria-live=polite (donate.html:219).

top_findings.title: Exit-intent overlay declares aria-modal="true" but does not trap focus (WCAG 2.4.3, 1.3.1)
top_findings.evidence: index.html:420-431 (role="dialog" aria-modal="true") and inline script index.html:479-497 — only Escape handling and initial focus to the close button; no Tab containment, unlike the animal modal which has a real trap (app.js:542-584)
top_findings.detail: When the mouseleave-triggered donation dialog opens, Tab moves focus into the page behind it while ARIA tells screen readers the rest of the page is inert — a direct contradiction that strands users. It also appears unprompted on mouse movement, interrupting task flow. The codebase already contains a correct focus-trap implementation for the animal detail modal; it just isn't applied here.

top_findings.title: Gold focus indicator fails non-text contrast on light backgrounds (WCAG 1.4.11 / 2.4.13-adjacent)
top_findings.evidence: styles.css:964-968 (:focus { outline:none } then :focus-visible { outline: 3px solid var(--gold) }) and styles.css:1291; --gold is #c9a24b (styles.css:61), page backgrounds --cream #fbf8f1 / --bone #f4efe6
top_findings.detail: The sitewide keyboard focus ring is #c9a24b gold, which measures roughly 2.1-2.3:1 against the cream/bone backgrounds used on nearly every section — below the 3:1 minimum for focus indicators. Because :focus outlines are globally suppressed, this under-contrast ring is the only focus indication low-vision keyboard users get on buttons, cards, chips, and links across the site. On the dark pine sections the same gold passes comfortably, so a two-token approach is needed.

top_findings.title: Desktop nav dropdown: aria-expanded never updates and is hover/focus-within only (WCAG 4.1.2)
top_findings.evidence: app.js:176-197 — aria-expanded synced via MutationObserver watching the .open class, but on desktop the menu opens purely via CSS :hover / :focus-within (styles.css:223) so the class never changes; click on desktop navigates to about.html instead of toggling; chevron SVG at index.html:54 lacks aria-hidden
top_findings.detail: Screen-reader users are told the 'Our Story' disclosure is permanently collapsed even while the submenu is open, and touch/screen-reader users who 'click' the toggle are navigated away rather than shown the menu (Ways to Help, Corporate Giving, Press, FAQ). The mobile code path is correct; the desktop path relies entirely on CSS state that ARIA cannot see. Note accessibility.html:58-63 also has a divergent copy of this submenu (missing Ways to Help), showing header markup is drifting across the 20+ page copies.

top_findings.title: Accessibility statement is stale, targets WCAG 2.1, and lacks a working email contact
top_findings.evidence: accessibility.html:121 ('We aim to meet ... WCAG 2.1 Level AA'); accessibility.html:127 says 'send us an email' but provides no address; duplicate <h1> at accessibility.html:80 and accessibility.html:101; 'Last updated: June 2026'
top_findings.detail: The statement predates WCAG 2.2 (relevant new AA criteria like 2.4.11 Focus Not Obscured, 2.5.8 Target Size, 3.3.8 Accessible Authentication apply to this site's sticky bars and admin login). It invites email feedback without giving an email, and the page renders two h1 elements with near-identical text. As the public conformance claim, this should be the easiest thing to get right — and it currently overstates conformance given the form findings above.

revamp_recommendations.title: Replace the adopt/foster multi-step forms with the volunteer-signup form engine
revamp_recommendations.detail: volunteer-signup.html (lines ~360-560) is the best-built form in the ecosystem: real fieldset/legend per step, for/id labels, aria-required, aria-invalid, role=alert errors, aria-pressed pill buttons, autocomplete tokens, reduced-motion handling — and it even asks applicants about accessibility accommodations. Generalize that renderer into a shared, schema-driven form module (steps defined as JSON) and use it for adopt-apply.html and foster-apply.html, deleting their broken bespoke step logic. This fixes findings 2 and 3 wholesale and gives SanctuaryBase a single form schema it can also render for in-person/kiosk intake.

revamp_recommendations.title: Make website applications first-class records in SanctuaryBase, not contact-inbox messages
revamp_recommendations.detail: Add typed Worker endpoints (POST /api/applications/adopt|foster|volunteer) that the forms submit to, and sync them into SanctuaryBase's existing applications store (SB- v2/src/stores/applications.js) via the site proxy — including the animal ID when an applicant clicked a specific animal card. Then close the loop with an accessible applicant status page or email updates ('received → phone call → meet-and-greet'), replacing today's dead-end where the only confirmation is an innerHTML blob (adopt-apply.html:499-501). Structured data also lets SanctuaryBase's people/donors/contacts modules dedupe applicants against existing volunteers and donors.

revamp_recommendations.title: Add an alt-text and accessibility gate to the animal publishing pipeline
revamp_recommendations.detail: The website's known weakest content is volunteer-uploaded animal photos (admitted in accessibility.html:124). In SanctuaryBase's animalsAdmin flow (SB- v2/src/stores/animalsAdmin.js → site proxy → /admin/animals in _worker.js), make a descriptive alt-text field required before an animal can be set to 'available', with guidance text ('describe what the animal looks like and is doing'). Store it in the animal record and render it in animalCard() (app.js:136) instead of falling back to the bare name. This turns the internal app into the enforcement point for public-site accessibility — exactly the 'front door' relationship the revamp envisions.

revamp_recommendations.title: Fix the four code-level WCAG failures on the money pages
revamp_recommendations.detail: (1) Name the monthly toggle: wrap it as <label>Monthly giving<input...></label> or add aria-label plus aria-checked switch role, and reflect state in text, donate.html:224-232. (2) Give the exit-intent overlay the same focus trap as the animal modal (reuse app.js:571-583) or replace it with a non-modal banner. (3) Make the adopt-page animal grid and volunteer time-selector real buttons/links with keyboard support (adopt-apply.html:402, app.js:444-457). (4) Wire initInlineValidation to all forms, not just #contact-form (app.js:485).

revamp_recommendations.title: Introduce a dual-tone focus indicator token and re-audit contrast
revamp_recommendations.detail: Define --focus-ring-on-light (e.g. the existing --pine #1f3a2e or --clay #b5623a, both >3:1 on cream/bone) and keep gold for dark pine sections; apply via section-tone classes. While in styles.css, also check the dark-mode palette block (styles.css:~810+) which remaps --cream/--ink and has never been contrast-verified, and the .toggle-label active/inactive opacity states on donate.html.

revamp_recommendations.title: Templatize the shared header/footer to stop accessibility drift
revamp_recommendations.detail: Twenty-plus HTML pages each carry a hand-copied header, dropdown, newsletter strip, and footer; they are already diverging (accessibility.html's submenu differs from index.html's; donate.html's <main> lacks the tabindex="-1" other pages have at line 72 of index.html). Move to build-time includes (even a simple 11ty/scripts step before Pages deploy) so one fix — e.g. the dropdown aria-expanded repair — lands everywhere at once. This is the single cheapest way to keep the site auditable as it grows.

revamp_recommendations.title: Update the accessibility statement to WCAG 2.2 AA with a real feedback channel
revamp_recommendations.detail: Revise accessibility.html: target WCAG 2.2 AA, list the actual known issues from this review (transparency builds trust), add a monitored email address (admin@ or a dedicated accessibility@ alias via the site's Cloudflare email routing), remove the duplicate h1 (lines 80/101), and date-stamp reviews. Consider routing accessibility feedback into SanctuaryBase's contacts inbox with a dedicated subject tag so it is triaged like other operational work.

revamp_recommendations.title: Extend the audit to SanctuaryBase itself before the revamp
revamp_recommendations.detail: This review covered the public website; the internal Vue app (SB- v2, ~40 stores, admin/features components) is a daily work tool for volunteers — including, per the volunteer form's own accommodation question, volunteers with disabilities. Before rebuilding it into the all-in-one platform, audit its DESIGN_SYSTEM.md tokens for contrast, its form components for label/error semantics, and its data tables and clock-in/rounds flows for keyboard operability, so the powerhouse is not built on the same gaps found here.

strengths: Skip link on every page with a correctly focusable target (<main id="main-content" tabindex="-1">, index.html:39,72) and a well-styled :focus reveal (styles.css:796-808)
A shared screen-reader live region with SF.announce()/SF.toast() (app.js:67-98) actually used by the newsletter and name-suggestion flows — rare to see in a hand-built static site
Proper focus trap and focus-return implementation for the animal detail modal (app.js:541-584), a pattern ready to be reused for the exit-intent dialog

volunteer-signup.html's JS form engine is genuinely excellent: fieldsets/legends per step, aria-invalid, role=alert errors, aria-pressed pills, autocomplete tokens — and it proactively asks applicants about accessibility accommodations
Extensive prefers-reduced-motion coverage (15+ media blocks in styles.css; count-up override in app.js:474-481; noscript reveal fallback in every <head>)

Consistent decorative-SVG hygiene (aria-hidden="true" on nearly all inline icons), labeled icon-only buttons (nav-toggle with aria-expanded/aria-controls, carousel prev/next, share buttons), and footer links underlined on hover/focus so they aren't color-only (styles.css:1432-1434)
Honeypot spam protection (adopt-apply.html:156) instead of a CAPTCHA — avoids the WCAG 3.3.8 accessible-authentication trap entirely
Server-side role-verified admin proxy (SB- v2/functions/api/site/[[path]].js) keeps the public site free of any admin UI, so the public surface stays simple and auditable

expert: Knowbility-style nonprofit accessibility auditor (WCAG 2.2 AA lens, screen reader + keyboard + mobile field-use focus)
integration_assessment: The two systems are partially connected, one-directionally. The app publishes animal records to the website's Neon-backed listings via src/stores/animalsAdmin.js and services/animalsAdminService (each internal animal tracks a siteAnimalId; importFromSite() reconciles pre-existing site animals), and the website's app.js explicitly notes "all admin operations... are performed through the SanctuaryBase app, which proxies to this site's /api/admin/* routes." However, the inbound flows are broken: the website stores adoption/foster/volunteer applications, donations, and newsletter signups in its own Postgres database via _worker.js, while the app's applications store (src/stores/applications.js) reads a completely separate Firestore 'applications' collection — public applicants never appear in SanctuaryBase without manual re-entry, and the donors store has no link to website donation records. From an accessibility standpoint this split matters: applicant-facing status communication happens in neither system, and staff processing applications must juggle two UIs (admin.html and the app) with two different, inconsistent accessibility postures.

top_findings.title: Keyboard focus indicator removed on every button in the app
top_findings.evidence: src/components/ui/AppButton.vue line 42 (`outline: none` on .app-btn with no :focus or :focus-visible rule); src/style.css lines 377 and 762 (`outline: none` on .btn and inputs); a grep for `focus-visible` across src/ returns zero hits in any component or stylesheet
top_findings.detail: AppButton is the primitive used by nearly every page (AnimalDetail.vue, admin pages, modals), and it strips the browser outline without replacing it. Inputs get a focus box-shadow (style.css line 775, AppInput.vue line 128), but buttons, nav tabs, and the MoreDrawer feature buttons give a sighted keyboard user no indication of where focus is. This is a WCAG 2.4.7 failure across the entire app — keyboard-only staff and volunteers effectively cannot operate it. Ironically the token already exists: --shadow-focus is defined in design-tokens.css line 200 but never applied to a button.

top_findings.title: No modal in the app has dialog semantics, a focus trap, or Escape handling
top_findings.evidence: src/components/shared/ConfirmDialog.vue (plain divs, no role=dialog/aria-modal, no focus move on open, no keydown handler, no focus return); src/components/features/modals/DietModal.vue; the inline edit modal in src/components/features/AnimalDetail.vue lines 28-60; ~20 more position:fixed overlays across features/admin (GrantDetailModal.vue, PersonDetailDrawer.vue, DeleteMessageDialog.vue, etc.), none with keyboard handlers per grep of @keydown
top_findings.detail: When ConfirmDialog opens (used for every destructive action via ui.confirm()), keyboard focus stays on the page behind it; a screen reader user is never told a dialog opened, can tab into obscured background content, and cannot dismiss with Escape. The overlay's only dismissal besides the buttons is @click.self — mouse-only. Because every modal in the app is hand-rolled with the same divs, this is a systemic WCAG 2.4.3 / 4.1.2 failure, not a one-off.

top_findings.title: Toast notifications — the app's sole feedback channel — are invisible to screen readers
top_findings.evidence: src/components/shared/Toast.vue: the toast div has no aria-live, role=status, or role=alert; src/stores/ui.js showToast() (line 36) is the app-wide pattern for success AND error reporting per ARCHITECTURE.md's own component examples ('ui.showToast("Error: " + error.message, "error")')
top_findings.detail: Every save, delete, and failure in the app is communicated exclusively through this transient visual toast. A screen reader user who submits a feeding log, marks meds given, or hits a Firestore error receives zero announcement — they cannot tell whether their action succeeded. WCAG 4.1.3 (Status Messages) failure with high operational risk in a medical/animal-care context where a silently failed 'meds given' save has real consequences.

top_findings.title: AppInput/AppSelect labels are not programmatically associated; clear button is mouse-only
top_findings.evidence: src/components/ui/AppInput.vue lines 3-6 (label element with no `for`, input with no `id`), line 34 (clearable control is a div with @click only — no button role, no aria-label, no keyboard handler), lines 44-45 (error/hint divs not linked via aria-describedby, no aria-invalid); src/components/ui/AppSelect.vue lines 3-13 (same unassociated label; placeholder option uses hardcoded `selected` attribute alongside :value binding)
top_findings.detail: These two primitives back essentially every form in the app (DietModal, AnimalDetail edit modal, all admin pages). Screen readers announce each field as an unlabeled 'edit text', and validation errors render visually but are never announced or associated with the field (WCAG 1.3.1, 3.3.2, 4.1.2).
